question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Bucket.builder() with no SynchronizationStrategy (defaulting to unsynchronized local bucket) on rateLimitBucket to cause concurrent tryConsume(1) calls from multiple threads to read the same stale token count, all returning true simultaneously, effectively multiplying the allowed request rate and overloading 30%+ of processing nodes?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the sequential check order in throttle() — rateLimitBucket checked first, then gasLimitBucket — to craft requests that pass the rate check but fail the gas check, consuming rate tokens without consuming gas tokens, effectively wasting the rate budget for legitimate users while the attacker's gas-failing requests cost nothing in gas throttle terms?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the refillGreedy behavior — which distributes token refill as fast as possible rather than at fixed intervals — to predict exact refill timing and submit requests in synchronized bursts that always arrive when tokens are available, sustaining maximum throughput continuously and degrading 30%+ of processing node capacity?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that gasLimitBucket.addTokens() in restore() is called unconditionally when tokens > 0 — with no check that the restored amount doesn't exceed what was originally consumed — to trigger over-restoration by crafting requests where gasLimit is large but gasUsed is small, causing the bucket to accumulate more tokens than its capacity should allow?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: opcodeRateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the opcodeRateLimitBucket's lack of SynchronizationStrategy — while the gasLimitBucket uses SYNCHRONIZED — to race concurrent opcode requests past the single-token limit, causing the heavy opcode tracing endpoint to execute multiple times simultaneously and overload 30%+ of processing nodes?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the global rateLimitBucket shared across all nodes in a horizontally scaled deployment — where each node has its own independent bucket instance — to multiply the effective allowed RPS by the number of nodes, sending N*500 requests per second across N nodes without triggering any single node's throttle, degrading 30%+ of total network processing capacity?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the per-node independent gasLimitBucket instances in a horizontally scaled deployment to submit gas-maximizing requests to each node independently, consuming the full gas budget on each node simultaneously, degrading 30%+ of total network processing capacity without triggering any single node's throttle?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of connection-level or IP-level throttling in rateLimitBucket to use a single persistent HTTP/2 connection with multiplexed streams to send 500 concurrent requests simultaneously, consuming the entire per-second budget in a single network round-trip and starving all other users for the remainder of that second?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the scaleGas() truncation to submit requests with gas values just below the next 10,000 boundary (e.g., gas=29,999 consumes 2 tokens, same as gas=20,001) to maximize EVM execution per gas token, amplifying the computational load on processing nodes by up to 50% beyond what the throttle accounts for?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that rateLimitBucket uses Bucket.builder() without withSynchronizationStrategy() — defaulting to a non-thread-safe local bucket — to cause memory visibility issues in a multi-core JVM where token decrements on one CPU core are not immediately visible to other cores, allowing concurrent threads to all see a full bucket and bypass the rate limit?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: gasLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the SYNCHRONIZED strategy on gasLimitBucket — which uses Java's intrinsic lock — to cause priority inversion where a low-priority thread holds the gasLimitBucket lock during a slow restore() call, blocking all high-priority request threads from acquiring the lock and degrading 30%+ of processing node throughput?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/config/ThrottleConfiguration.java -> Scope: Medium: Shutdown of greater than or equal to 30% of network processing nodes without brute force actions, but does not shut down the network'] [Function: rateLimitBucket()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the refillGreedy(rateLimit, Duration.ofSeconds(1)) configuration — where the entire capacity refills in one second — to perform a sawtooth attack pattern that alternates between consuming all tokens and waiting exactly 1 second, sustaining maximum load indefinitely without triggering any anomaly detection that might look for sustained high rates?
